elements.sent-2411-001: Fragment-based drug discovery, in which molecular fragments are assembled into new molecules with desirable biochemical properties, has achieved great success.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, many fragment-based molecule generation methods show limited exploration beyond the existing fragments in the database as they only reassemble or slightly modify the given ones.
elements.sent-2411-003: To tackle this problem, we propose a new fragment-based molecule generation framework with retrieval augmentation, namely Fragment Retrieval-Augmented Generation (\name).
elements.sent-2411-004: Given a fragment vocabulary, \name retrieves two types of fragments: (1) hard fragments, which serve as building blocks that will be explicitly included in the newly generated molecule, and (2) soft fragments, which serve as reference to guide the generation of new fragments through a trainable fragment injection module.
elements.sent-2411-005: To extrapolate beyond the existing fragments, \name updates the fragment vocabulary with generated fragments via an iterative refinement process which is further enhanced with post-hoc genetic fragment modification.
elements.sent-2411-006: Fragment-based drug discovery (FBDD) has been considered as an effective approach to explore the chemical space and has resulted in many successful marketed drugs~\citep{li2020application .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Contrary to high-throughput screening (HTS)~\cite{walters1998virtual that searches from a library of drug-like molecules, FBDD constructs a library of molecular fragments to synthesize new molecules beyond the existing molecule library, leading to a better chemical coverage~\citep{bon2022fragment .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Recently, generative models have been adopted in the field of FBDD to accelerate the process of searching for drug candidates~\citep{jin2020multi,xie2020mars,yang2021hit,maziarz2021learning,kong2022molecule,geng2023novo,lee2023drug .}
elements.sent-2411-009: These methods reassemble or slightly modify the fragments to generate new molecules with the knowledge of the generative model.
elements.sent-2411-010: As the methods are allowed to exploit chemical knowledge in the form of fragments to narrow the search space, they have shown meaningful success in generating optimized molecules.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, their performance is largely limited by the existing library of molecular fragments, where discovering new fragments is either impossible or highly limited.
elements.sent-2411-012: Some of the methods~\cite{jensen2019graph,tripp2023genetic,lee2023drug suggested making modifications to existing fragments, but the modifications are only applied to small and local substructures and still heavily dependent on the existing fragments.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The limited exploration beyond known fragments greatly hinders the possibility of generating diverse and novel drug candidates that may exhibit better target properties.
elements.sent-2411-014: Therefore, it is a challenge to improve the ability of discovering novel high-quality fragments, while exploiting existing chemical space effectively.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures_pdf/concept.pdf} \caption{ The overall framework of \name. After an initial fragment vocabulary is constructed from an existing molecule library, two types of fragments are retrieved during generation. Hard fragments are explicitly included in the newly generated molecules, while soft fragments implicitly guide the generation of new fragments. SAFE-GPT generates a molecule using hard fragments as input, while the fragment injection module in the middle of the SAFE-GPT layers injects the embeddings of soft fragments into the input embedding. After the generation, the molecule population and fragment vocabulary are updated with the newly generated molecule and its fragments, respectively. The exploration is further enhanced with genetic fragment modification, which also updates the fragment vocabulary and molecule population.} \label{fig:concept}
elements.sent-2411-015: To this end, we propose a fragment-based molecule generation framework leveraging retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)~\cite{lewis2020retrieval , namely Fragment Retrieval-Augmented Generation (\name).}
elements.sent-2411-016: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:concept , \name augments the pre-trained molecular language model SAFE-GPT~\citep{noutahi2024gotta with two types of retrieved fragments: hard fragments and soft fragments for a better exploration-exploitation trade-off.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Specifically, we first construct a fragment vocabulary by decomposing known molecules from the existing library into fragments and scoring the fragments by measuring their contribution to target properties.
elements.sent-2411-018: From the fragment vocabulary, \name first retrieves fragments that will be explicitly included in the new molecule (i.e., hard fragments).
elements.sent-2411-019: Hard fragments serve as the input context to the molecular language model that predicts the remaining fragments.
elements.sent-2411-020: In addition to retrieval of hard fragments, \name retrieves fragments that will not be part of the generated molecule but provide informative guidance on predicting novel, diverse molecules (i.e., soft fragments).
elements.sent-2411-021: Concretely, the embeddings of soft fragments are fused with the embeddings of hard fragments (or input embeddings) through a lightweight fragment injection module in the middle of SAFE-GPT.
elements.sent-2411-022: The fragment injection module allows SAFE-GPT to generate new fragments by referring to the information conveyed by soft fragments.
elements.sent-2411-023: During training, we only update the fragment injection module while keeping SAFE-GPT frozen.
elements.sent-2411-024: Inspired by \citet{wang2023retrieval , we train \name to learn how to leverage the retrieved fragments for molecule generation, using a self-supervised loss that predicts the most similar one in the set of soft fragments.}
elements.sent-2411-025: At inference, \name dynamically updates the fragment vocabulary with newly generated fragments via an iterative refinement process.
elements.sent-2411-026: To further enhance exploration, we propose to modify the generated fragments from SAFE-GPT with a post-hoc genetic fragment modification process.
elements.sent-2411-027: The proposed \name takes the advantages of both hard and soft fragment retrieval to achieve an improved exploration-exploitation trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-028: We verify \name on various molecular optimization tasks, by examining the optimization performance, along with diversity, novelty, and synthesizability.
elements.sent-2411-029: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:radar , \name exhibits the best balance across these essential considerations, demonstrating its applicability as a promising tool for drug discovery.}
elements.sent-2411-030: We summarize our contributions as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-031: We introduce \name, a novel molecular generative framework that combines FBDD and RAG.
elements.sent-2411-032: We propose a retrieval augmentation strategy that operates at the fragment level with two types of fragments, allowing fine-grained guidance to achieve an improved exploration-exploitation trade-off and generate high-quality drug candidates.
elements.sent-2411-033: Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate the effectiveness of \name in various drug discovery tasks that simulate real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-034: Fragment-based molecular generative models refer to a class of methods that reassemble existing molecular substructures (i.e., fragments) to generate new molecules.
elements.sent-2411-035: On the other hand, graph-based GAs~\citep{jensen2019graph,tripp2023genetic decompose parent molecules into fragments that are combined to generate an offspring molecule, and are also mutated with a small probability.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Since fragment-based strategies are limited by generating molecules outside of the possible combinations of existing fragments, they suffer from limited exploration in the chemical space.
elements.sent-2411-037: Some of the methods~\cite{jensen2019graph,tripp2023genetic,lee2023drug suggest making modifications to existing fragments to overcome this problem, but this is not a fundamental solution because the modifications are only local, still being based on the existing fragments.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)~\citep{lewis2020retrieval refers to a technique that retrieves context from external data databases to guide the generation of a generative model.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Recently, RAG has gained attention as a means to enhance accuracy and reliability of large language models (LLMs)~\cite{borgeaud2022improving,ram2023context,asai2023retrieval .}
elements.sent-2411-040: In the field of molecule optimization, \citet{liu2023conversational developed a text-based drug editing framework that repurposes a conversational language model for solving molecular tasks, where the domain knowledge is injected by a retrieval and feedback module.}
elements.sent-2411-041: More related to us, \citet{wang2023retrieval proposed to use a pre-trained molecular language model to generate molecules, while augmenting the generation with retrieved molecules that have high target properties.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Contrary to this method that retrieves molecules, our proposed \name employs a fine-grained retrieval augmentation scheme that operates at the fragment level to achieve an improved exploration-exploitation trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-043: In this section, we introduce our Fragment Retrieval-Augmented Generation (\name) framework.
elements.sent-2411-044: We first introduce the hard fragment retrieval in Sec.~\ref{sec:hard .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Then, we present the soft fragment retrieval in Sec.~\ref{sec:soft .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Lastly, we describe the genetic fragment modification in Sec.~\ref{sec:genetic .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Given a set of $N$ molecules $x_i$ and their corresponding properties $y_i \in [0,1]$, denoted as $\mathcal{D =\{(x_i, y_i)\}^N_{i=1}$, we first construct a fragment vocabulary.}
elements.sent-2411-048: We adopt an arm-linker-arm slicing algorithm provided by \citet{noutahi2024gotta which decomposes a molecule $x$ into three fragments: two arms $F_\text{arm}$ (i.e., fragments that have one attachment point) and one linker $F_\text{linker}$ (i.e., a fragment that has two attachment points).}
elements.sent-2411-049: Decomposing molecules into arms and linkers (or scaffolds) is a popular approach utilized in various drug discovery strategies, such as scaffold decoration or scaffold hopping~\citep{schneider1999scaffold .}
elements.sent-2411-050: We ignored the molecules in the training set that cannot be decomposed, and a set of arms $\mathcal{F _\text{arm =\{F_{\text{arm ,j}\}^{2N _{j=1}$ and a set of linkers $\mathcal{F _\text{linker =\{F_{\text{linker ,j}\}^{N _{j=1}$ are obtained after the algorithm is applied to the molecules $\{x_i\}^N_{i=1}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Subsequently, we calculate the score of each fragment $F_j \in \mathcal{F _\text{arm \cup \mathcal{F _\text{linker}$ using the average property of all molecules containing $F_j$ as their substructure as follows: \looseness=-1 \begin{equation}\text{score}(F_j)= \frac{1}{\lvert S(F_j)\rvert} \sum_{(x,y)\in S(F_j)}y, \label{eq:score}\end{equation} where score$(F_j) \in [0, 1]$, and $S(F_j)=\{(x,y)\in\mathcal{D : F_j \text{ is a fragment of x\}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Intuitively, the fragment score evaluates the contribution of a given fragment to the target property of the whole molecule of which it is a part.
elements.sent-2411-053: From $\mathcal{F _\text{arm}$ and $\mathcal{F _\text{linker}$, we choose the top-$N_\text{frag}$ fragments based on the score to construct the arm fragment vocabulary $\mathcal{V _\text{arm}\subset \mathcal{F _\text{arm}$ and the linker fragment vocabulary $\mathcal{V _\text{linker}\subset \mathcal{F _\text{linker}$, respectively.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Given the fragment vocabularies $\mathcal{V _\text{arm}$ and $\mathcal{V _\text{linker}$ consisting of high-property fragments, two hard fragments are randomly retrieved from the vocabularies.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The hard fragments together form a partial molecular sequence that serves as input to a pre-trained molecular language model.
elements.sent-2411-056: In this work, we employ Sequential Attachment-based Fragment Embedding (SAFE)~\citep{noutahi2024gotta as the molecular representation and SAFE-GPT~\citep{noutahi2024gotta as the backbone generative model of \name.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: SAFE is a non-canonical version of simplified molecular-input line-entry system (SMILES)~\citep{weininger1988smiles that represents molecules as a sequence of dot-connected fragments.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Importantly, the order of fragments in a SAFE string does not affect the molecular identity.
elements.sent-2411-059: Using the SAFE representation, \name forces the hard fragments to be included in a newly generated molecule by providing them as an input sequence to the language model to complete the rest of the sequence.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{figures_pdf/hard.pdf} \caption{ Hard fragment retrieval of \name. With a probability of $50\%$, \name either retrieves two arms as hard fragments for linker design (top) or one arm and one linker as hard fragments for motif extension (bottom).} \label{fig:hard}
elements.sent-2411-060: During generation, with a probability of $50\%$, \name either (1) retrieves two hard fragments from $\mathcal{V _\text{arm}$ or (2) retrieves one from $\mathcal{V _\text{arm}$ and one from $\mathcal{V _\text{linker}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: In the former case, \name performs linker design, which generates a new fragment that links the input fragments.
elements.sent-2411-062: In the latter case, \name first randomly selects an attachment point in the retrieved linker and combines it with the retrieved arm to form a single fragment, and then performs motif extension, which generates a new fragment that completes the molecule (Figure~\ref{fig:hard ).}
elements.sent-2411-063: Given two hard fragments as input, the molecular language model generates one new fragment to complete a molecule.
elements.sent-2411-064: Instead of relying solely on the model to generate the new fragment, we propose to augment the generation with the information of $K$ retrieved fragments, which we refer to as soft fragments, to guide the generation.
elements.sent-2411-065: Specifically, if the two hard fragments are all arms, \name randomly retrieves soft fragments from $\mathcal{V _\text{linker}$.}
elements.sent-2411-066: If one of the hard fragments is an arm and another is a linker, \name randomly retrieves soft fragments from $\mathcal{V _\text{arm}$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Using up to the $L$-th layer of the lauguage model $\text{LM ^{0:L}$, the embeddings of the input sequence $x_\text{input}$ and the soft fragments $\{F_{\text{soft ,k}\}^K_{k=1}$ are obtained as follows: \begin{equation} h_input=LM^{0:L}(x_input) \,\,\, and \,\,\, H_soft=concatenate([h^1_soft,h^2_soft,\dots,h^K_soft]), where \,\,\, h^k_soft=LM^{0:L}(F_{soft,k})  for  k{=}1,2,\dots,K. \end{equation} Subsequently, \name injects the embeddings of soft fragments through a trainable fragment injection module.}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation} h_input=LM^{0:L}(x_input) \,\,\, and \,\,\, H_soft=concatenate([h^1_soft,h^2_soft,\dots,h^K_soft]), where \,\,\, h^k_soft=LM^{0:L}(F_{soft,k})  for  k{=}1,2,\dots,K. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-068: Following \citet{wang2023retrieval , the fragment injection module uses cross-attention to fuse the embeddings of the input sequence and soft fragments as follows: \begin{equation}\bm{h} = \text{FI}(\bm{h}_\text{input}, \bm{H}_\text{soft}) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{\text{Query}(\bm{h}_\text{input}) \cdot \text{Key}(\bm{H}_\text{soft})^\top}{\sqrt{d_\text{Key}}}\right) \cdot \text{Value}(\bm{H}_\text{soft}),\end{equation} where FI is the fragment injection module, Query, Key, and Value are multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs), and $d_\text{Key}$ is the output dimension of Key.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Next, a molecule is generated by decoding the augmented embedding $\bm{h}$ using the later layers of the language model as $x_\text{new =\text{LM ^{L+1:L_T (\bm{h )$, where $L_T$ is the total number of layers of the model.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: With the fragment injection module, \name can utilize information of soft fragments to generate novel fragments which are also likely to contribute to the high target properties.
elements.sent-2411-071: During generation, the fragment vocabulary is dynamically updated through an iterative process that scores newly generated fragments based on Eq.~\eqref{eq:score and replaces fragments in the fragment vocabulary to the top-$N_\text{frag}$ fragments.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures_pdf/train.pdf} \caption{ The self-supervised training process of the fragment injection module of \name. $F^{k\text{NN}}$ denotes the $k$-th most similar fragment to $F$. Using $F_1$ and $F_2$ as hard fragments, while using $F_3$ and its neighbors $\{F^{kNN}_3\}^K_{k=2}$ as soft fragments, the training objective is to predict $F^{1NN}_3$.} \label{fig:train}
elements.sent-2411-072: Next, we need to train \name to learn how to augment the retrieved soft fragments into the molecule generation.
elements.sent-2411-073: To retain the high generation quality of SAFE-GPT and make the training process efficient, we keep the backbone language model frozen and only train the lightweight fragment injection module.
elements.sent-2411-074: Inspired by \citet{wang2023retrieval , we propose a new self-supervised objective that predicts the most similar fragment to the input fragments.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Specifically, each molecular sequence $x$ in the training set is first decomposed into fragment sequences $(F_1,F_2,F_3)$ with a random permutation between the fragments, using the same slicing algorithm used in the vocabulary construction.
elements.sent-2411-076: Importantly, using the SAFE representation, $x$ can be simply represented by connecting its fragments with dots as $F_1.F_2.F_3$.

elements.sent-2411-078: Given the remaining fragment $F_3$, we retrieve its $K$ most similar fragments $\{F_3^{kNN}\}_{k=1 ^K$ from the training fragment pool.}

elements.sent-2411-085: To further enhance exploration in the chemical space, we propose to modify the generated fragments with a post-hoc genetic algorithm (GA).
elements.sent-2411-086: Specifically, we adopt the operations of Graph GA~\citep{jensen2019graph .}

elements.sent-2411-092: Through this interplay of hard fragment retrieval, soft fragment retrieval, and the genetic fragment modification, \name can exploit existing chemical knowledge through the form of fragments both explicitly and implicitly, while exploring beyond initial fragments by the dynamic vocabulary update.

elements.sent-2411-142: The detailed results are shown in Table~\ref{tab:ablation_auc and Table~\ref{tab:ablation_div_nov_sa .}}
elements.sent-2411-143: For \name (soft+GA) that does not utilize the hard fragment retrieval, we randomly initialized the fragment vocabularies instead of selecting the top-$N_\text{frag}$ fragments based on Eq.~\eqref{eq:score and let the model use random hard fragments.}
elements.sent-2411-144: Note that even though \name (hard+soft), \name that does not utilize the genetic fragment modification, shows very high diversity, the value is not meaningful as the generated molecules are not highly optimized, not novel, and not synthesizable.
elements.sent-2411-145: The same applies to the high novelty of \name (soft+GA) and \name (GA).
elements.sent-2411-146: On the contrary, the full \name achieves the best optimization performance and synthesizability while showing reasonably high diversity and novelty.
elements.sent-2411-147: Importantly, \name outperforms \name (hard+GA) in all the metrics, especially for diversity and novelty, indicating the soft fragment retrieval aids the model in generating more diverse and novel drug candidates while maintaining their high target properties.
elements.sent-2411-148: It is well-known that molecular diversity is important in drug discovery, especially when the oracle has noise.
elements.sent-2411-149: However, optimization performance against the target property and molecular diversity are conflicting factors~\citep{gao2022sample,kim2024genetic .}
elements.sent-2411-150: To control this trade-off between optimization performance and diversity, we additionally introduce a similarity-based fragment filtering strategy, which excludes similar fragments from the fragment vocabulary.
elements.sent-2411-151: Specifically, when updating the vocabulary, new fragments that have a higher Tanimoto similarity than $\delta$ to fragments in the vocabulary are filtered out.
elements.sent-2411-152: Morgan fingerprints of radius 2 and 1024 bits are used to calculate the Tanimoto similarity.
elements.sent-2411-153: The results of the similarity-based fragment filter with different values of $\delta$ are shown in Figure~\ref{fig:ablation_simfilter (c), verifying \name can increase diversity at the expense of optimization performance by controlling $\delta$.}
elements.paragraph-2411-009: Effect of the fragment vocabulary update.
elements.sent-2411-154: To analyze the effect of the dynamic update of the fragment vocabulary during generation, we visualize the distribution of the docking scores of molecules generated by \name with or with the fragment vocabulary update in Figure~\ref{fig:kde .}
elements.sent-2411-155: The dynamic update allows \name to generate more optimized molecules in terms of the target property.
elements.sent-2411-156: Notably, with the dynamic update, \name can discover molecules that have higher binding affinity to the target protein than the top molecule in the training set, and we visualize an example of such molecules in the figure.
elements.sent-2411-157: Note that the molecule also has low similarity ($0.321$) with the training molecules.
elements.sent-2411-158: This result demonstrates the explorability of \name to discover drug candidates that lie beyond the training distribution.
elements.sent-2411-159: We proposed \name, a new fragment-based molecular generative framework that utilizes hard fragment retrieval, soft fragment retrieval, and genetic fragment modification.
elements.sent-2411-160: With hard fragment retrieval, \name can explicitly exploit the information contained in existing fragments that contribute to the target properties.
elements.sent-2411-161: With soft fragment retrieval, \name can balance between exploitation of existing chemical knowledge and exploration beyond the existing fragment vocabulary.
elements.sent-2411-162: Soft fragment retrieval with SAFE-GPT allows \name to propose new fragments that are likely to contribute to the target chemical properties.
elements.sent-2411-163: The proposed novel fragments are then dynamically incorporated in the fragment vocabulary throughout generation.
elements.sent-2411-164: This exploration is further enhanced with genetic fragment modification, which modifies fragments with genetic operations and updates the vocabulary.
elements.sent-2411-165: Through extensive experiments, we demonstrated the efficacy of \name to improve the exploration-exploitation trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-166: We would like to thank the NVIDIA GenAIR team for their comments during the development of the framework.. .
elements.sent-2411-167: Since our proposed \name is built on a pre-trained backbone molecular language model, it relies heavily on the generation performance of the backbone model.
elements.sent-2411-168: However, this also means that our method delegates the difficult task of molecule generation to a large model and lets the lightweight fragment injection module take care of the relatively easy task of fragment retrieval augmentation.
elements.sent-2411-169: This strategy enables very efficient and fast training (Section~\ref{sec:resources ) and makes \name a simple but powerful method to solve various drug discovery tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-170: Through our paper, we demonstrated that \name can achieve improved trade-offs between various considerations in drug discovery, showing its strong applicability to real-world drug discovery tasks.
elements.sent-2411-171: However, given its effectiveness, \name has the possibility to be used maliciously to generate harmful molecules.
elements.sent-2411-172: This can be prevented by setting the target properties to comprehensively consider toxicity and other side effects, or filtering out toxic fragments from the fragment vocabulary during generation.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{Generation Process of \name} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Dataset $\mathcal{D}$, fragment vocabulary size $N_\text{frag}$, molecule population size $N_\text{mol}$, \quad\quad\quad number of soft fragments $K$, number of total generations $G$, \quad\quad\quad number of SAFE-GPT generations per cycle $G_\text{SAFE-GPT}$, \quad\quad\quad number of GA generations per cycle $G_\text{GA}$ \STATE Set $\mathcal{V}_\text{arm} \gets$ top-$N_\text{frag}$ arms obtained from $\mathcal{D}$ (Eq.~\eqref{eq:score}) \STATE Set $\mathcal{V}_\text{linker} \gets$ top-$N_\text{frag}$ linkers obtained from $\mathcal{D}$ (Eq.~\eqref{eq:score}) \STATE Set $\mathcal{P} \gets \emptyset$ \STATE Set $\mathcal{M} \gets \emptyset$ \WHILE{$\vert\mathcal{M}\vert < G$} \STATE $\triangleright$ Fragment retrieval-augmented SAFE-GPT generation \FOR{$i=1,2,\dots,N_\text{SAFE-GPT}$} \STATE Randomly retrieve two hard fragments $F_{\text{hard},1},F_{\text{hard},2}$ from $\mathcal{V}_\text{arm} \cup \mathcal{V}_\text{linker}$ \STATE Randomly retrieve $K$ soft fragments $\{F_{\text{soft}}\}_{k=1}^{K}$ from $\mathcal{V}_\text{arm} \cup \mathcal{V}_\text{linker}$ \STATE Using $F_{\text{hard},1}.F_{\text{hard},2}$ as input and $\{F_{\text{soft}}\}_{k=1}^{K}$ as soft fragments, run SAFE-GPT to generate a molecule $x$ \STATE Update $\mathcal{M} \gets \mathcal{M} \cup \{x\}$ \tikzmark{top} \STATE Decompose $x$ into $F_{\text{arm},1},F_\text{linker}$, and $F_{\text{arm},2}$ \STATE Update $\mathcal{V}_\text{arm} \gets$ top-$N_\text{frag}$ arms from $\mathcal{V}_\text{arm} \cup \{F_{\text{arm},1},F_{\text{arm},2}\}$ \tikzmark{right} \STATE Update $\mathcal{V}_\text{linker} \gets$ top-$N_\text{frag}$ linkers from $\mathcal{V}_\text{linker} \cup F_\text{linker}$ \STATE Update $\mathcal{P} \gets$ top-$N_\text{mol}$ from $\mathcal{P} \cup \{x\}$ \tikzmark{bottom} \AddNote{top}{bottom}{right}{Update} \ENDFOR \STATE $\triangleright$ GA generation \FOR{$i=1,2,\dots,N_\text{GA}$} \STATE Select parent molecules from $\mathcal{P}$ \STATE Perform crossover and mutation to generate a molecule $x$ \STATE Update $\mathcal{M} \gets \mathcal{M} \cup \{x\}$ \tikzmark{top} \STATE Decompose $x$ into $F_{\text{arm},1},F_\text{linker}$, and $F_{\text{arm},2}$ \STATE Update $\mathcal{V}_\text{arm} \gets$ top-$N_\text{frag}$ arms from $\mathcal{V}_\text{arm} \cup \{F_{\text{arm},1},F_{\text{arm},2}\}$ \tikzmark{right} \STATE Update $\mathcal{V}_\text{linker} \gets$ top-$N_\text{frag}$ linkers from $\mathcal{V}_\text{linker} \cup F_\text{linker}$ \STATE Update $\mathcal{P} \gets$ top-$N_\text{mol}$ from $\mathcal{P} \cup \{x\}$ \tikzmark{bottom} \AddNote{top}{bottom}{right}{Update} \ENDFOR \ENDWHILE \STATE Output: Generated molecules $\mathcal{M}$ \end{algorithmic} \label{tab:algorithm}
